title: rung-3 cursor advance delegated to nested runVerifySpot — couples + double-loads
description: After the rung-3 snapshot+overwrite+saveState block, requestHint dispatches the entire runVerifySpot tool handler in-process (not just runVerification). runVerifySpot then re-runs probeOutputStyle, re-loads state from disk (which was just written one statement earlier), re-loads phases, re-fetches getCurrentSpot, re-checks the verification spec, runs runVerification, advances cursor, and saves state again. So the rung-3 happy path performs: 1 outputStyleOk gate (requestHint) + 1 outputStyleOk gate (verifySpot, redundant) + 2 loadState round-trips + 2 loadPhases round-trips + 2 saveState commits. The contract said 'dispatches runVerifySpot' but the right primitive to compose is runVerification (or an internal advance helper), not the whole tool. The synthesis-notes already flagged the operational consequence: T-095 had a 'stale-state bug' because the test mock could not model the disk round-trip between requestHint's saveState and verifySpot's loadState — production correctness depends on disk consistency, which is fragile.
impact: Two concurrent failure modes: (a) if the second saveState (inside verifySpot) fails after the first one succeeded, auto_completed is on disk but the cursor advance silently rolled back — the response says advanced:false but disk and response agree only by accident; (b) outputStyleOk could legitimately flip to false between requestHint's gate and verifySpot's redundant gate (a settings.json edit), surfacing 'output-style-disabled' inside autoVerifyResult.error in a way callers won't expect. The double-IO also doubles the disk-failure surface that M002 was trying to harden.
recommendation: Compose primitives, not tools. Inside requestHint after the auto-write+saveState succeeds, call runVerification(spec, projectRoot, opts) directly, then on pass call advanceCursor(state-with-rung3-flags, phasesData) once and saveState once — single transactional commit covering both auto_completed AND cursor advance. Keep runVerifySpot as the public entry for the verifySpot tool. Bonus: the dead 'savedState' / dead 'advanceCursor' import become live and meaningful again.
evidence:
// (e) Dispatch runVerifySpot to auto-verify the written file
  const verifyResult = await runVerifySpot({ projectRoot }, opts);

title: advanceCursor and savedState are imported/declared but never used
description: Line 4 imports `advanceCursor` from phaseEngine.js; the symbol is referenced nowhere in the file. Line 266 declares `let savedState = newState;` and the variable is never read again — `newState` is the only thing used past that point. Both are byproducts of the design tension flagged in R2-001: the author started threading cursor-advance into requestHint locally (which would have used advanceCursor and the savedState binding meaningfully), then pivoted to delegating the whole flow to runVerifySpot, but didn't clean up the leftover symbols. tsc --noEmit under strict:true should be flagging the unused import; the project's tsconfig must have noUnusedLocals/noUnusedParameters off.
impact: Dead symbols are a clear signal of an incomplete refactor. Future maintainers reading line 4 will assume requestHint owns its own cursor advance (it doesn't — verifySpot does); reading line 266 will assume there's a re-save of savedState somewhere (there isn't). Wastes review time on every future edit. Also a hint that strict-mode lints are loose, which compounds across the codebase.
recommendation: Drop `advanceCursor` from the imports, drop the `let savedState = newState;` declaration. Tighten tsconfig with noUnusedLocals: true so the next dead-symbol regression fails CI on its own.

title: AutoWriteFailedWarning uses non-standard 'kind_detail' field; breaks discriminated-union convention
description: Every other warning interface in this file follows the discriminated-union convention `kind: '<literal>'` as the unique discriminator, plus message + kind-specific scalar fields. AutoWriteFailedWarning uniquely introduces a second field named `kind_detail` for what is really a sub-discriminator. The auto-write error space has TWO orthogonal axes (the warning-kind axis = 'auto-write-failed' and the failure-mode axis = target-file-missing | target-range-invalid | snapshot-write-failed | overwrite-failed), and the right encoding is either (a) four sibling literal kinds in the same union, or (b) a separate `reason: '...' ` field — `kind_detail` is neither, and TS callers narrowing on `w.kind === 'auto-write-failed'` won't get exhaustiveness checking on the four sub-modes.
impact: Breaks the symmetric pattern that lets reviewers/tools grep `kind: '...'` to enumerate the warning surface (cycle-4 A12 grep target). Future contributors will copy the pattern and accumulate ad-hoc sub-discriminators (kind_detail, kind_subkind, etc.). Loses TS exhaustiveness on a 4-branch space that genuinely needs it.
recommendation: Either expand the union to four kinds ('auto-write-target-file-missing', 'auto-write-target-range-invalid', 'auto-write-snapshot-write-failed', 'auto-write-overwrite-failed'), or rename `kind_detail` → `reason: AutoWriteErrorKind` to keep the four-mode field but stop colliding with the union convention. The AutoWriteError class in ladder.ts already has a `kind` field of the right shape; the warning interface should mirror it as `reason`, not as `kind_detail`.

title: Three new warning kinds are exported but never constructed — orphan union members
description: Cycle-5 added StateSaveFailedWarning, AutoWriteFailedWarning, OutputStyleDisabledWarning to EngineWarning. None of the production tool code actually constructs an EngineWarning literal of these kinds. selectPath.ts/setPersonalization.ts emit `errors: [\`state-save-failed: ${e.message}\`]` (a string in a string array, not an object with kind). verifySpot.ts emits `error: 'output-style-disabled'` (bare string). requestHint.ts emits its own discriminated RequestHintError shape (own kind union, own message). No path through the engine ever produces { kind: 'state-save-failed', message: ... } as a typed EngineWarning. This is the exact same shape that cycle-3 R1-003/R2-001/M002 + cycle-4 R2-002 (worker-2's previous review on me) flagged for RegistryWarning: declared union members with no producer.
impact: Defeats the discriminated-union promise: callers handling `EngineWarning` get type-narrowed to branches that runtime never reaches. Tests T-085/T-086/T-087 assert the literals are present in the source file via grep — passing those tests proves nothing about the producer surface. Continues a multi-cycle pattern of warnings.ts drifting away from registry.ts's actual emission surface.
recommendation: Either (a) wire requestHint, selectPath, setPersonalization, verifySpot to actually emit { kind: 'state-save-failed', message } / { kind: 'auto-write-failed', spotId, reason, message } / { kind: 'output-style-disabled', tool } as part of their structured failures (would also unify the four current ad-hoc shapes), or (b) drop the three new interfaces from EngineWarning until a real producer exists. Pick one; the current state is the worst of both — schema bloat without runtime use.

title: outputStyleOk gate produces four different failure shapes across five tools
description: Same gate, five tools, four distinct response shapes for the same condition: selectPath/setPersonalization → `{ ok: false, errors: ['output-style-disabled'] }`; nextSpot → `{ done: false, error: 'output-style-disabled' }`; verifySpot → `{ pass: false, error: 'output-style-disabled', advanced: false }`; requestHint → `{ ok: false, error: { kind: 'output-style-disabled', message: '…' } }`. The skill (and the course-conductor agent that calls these) has to encode the shape switch per tool. The gate body itself is also copy-pasted five times with subtly different message strings; one site uses 'output-style-disabled: learning-output-style plugin is not enabled' (requestHint) and the others use the bare kind literal.
impact: Conductor logic that wants to detect 'gate failed and tell the student to enable the plugin' has to know four shapes. A single helper would have caught the pattern; instead it's been pasted with drift. Cycle-4 R2-004 already flagged the four-tool state-load preamble as a duplication hazard; cycle 5 added a fifth tool and made the duplication five-fold without abstracting.
recommendation: Extract `assertOutputStyleOk()` returning a `{ ok: true } | { ok: false; warning: OutputStyleDisabledWarning }` and let each tool map the warning to its own shape via one liner. Better yet, give all five tools a uniform 'gate failed' shape (e.g., always `{ ok: false, gate: 'output-style-disabled' }` as an additive field) so the skill can treat the gate generically. Tightening this is also necessary to make the orphan OutputStyleDisabledWarning from R2-004 actually live.

title: snapshot rotation timestamp doesn't reuse state.ts's collision-counter pattern
description: state.ts solved exactly this problem in cycle 2 (`state.corrupt-<ts>-<counter>.json` with a monotonic archiveCounter for same-millisecond collisions). ladder.ts's `.bak` rotation reaches for `new Date().toISOString().replace(/:/g, '')` and stops there — no counter, no random suffix. Two rapid rung-3 retries inside the same millisecond (plausible in tests; the contract's T-052 test pre-creates a .bak and then immediately calls runAutoWrite, depending on Date.now() advancing) collide on the rotated path; state.ts's wx flag would then make the second rename throw EEXIST, which the catch-block at line 196-204 reports as 'snapshot-write-failed' even though the failure is a rotation race, not a write failure. Existing test passes only because the pre-existing fixture is created on a separate event-loop tick from the rotation.
impact: Low-frequency collision potential, but more importantly: the cycle-2 archive-rotation idiom is the right pattern and ladder.ts re-implemented a half-version of it instead of factoring out `archiveWithTimestamp(path)` into state.ts (or a shared util). The contract Behavior section explicitly described .bak rotation 'so prior rung-3 attempts stay recoverable' — the recoverability promise gets shakier when a same-ms collision raises 'snapshot-write-failed' and aborts the user's rung-3 invocation entirely.
recommendation: Extract `rotateWithTimestamp(filePath)` into state.ts (or a new `mcp/server/src/fsRotate.ts`) using the same `<ts>-<counter>` pattern as archiveCorruptFile. Both call sites (cycle-2 corrupt state, cycle-5 rung-3 .bak) use the helper. Bonus: the rotation can use rename instead of read+write (atomic) which ladder.ts is already doing, but the helper keeps the timestamp+counter logic in one place.

title: course-conductor.md narrates 'autoVerifyResult.advanced' as the success signal — contradicts contract
description: The contract Behavior section: 'On verification pass, the cursor advances exactly as in cycle 4's pass branch' and 'autoVerifyResult: { pass: true, advanced: true }' on the happy path; 'pass: false, advanced: false' on the failed path. Pass is the source of truth — `advanced` is a derived field that reflects whether the cursor was successfully moved (which can fail independently from verification: M002 carry-forward, where pass=true but saveState rejects, gives advanced=false). The conductor agent's narration says 'If the auto-verify passed (autoVerifyResult.advanced: true), announce success and proceed.' — wrong field. A genuine pass-but-state-save-failed lands the student in a state where the auto-verify passed (so the student's code is correct) but advanced is false (so the conductor announces failure and prompts another edit). Confusing UX.
impact: User-visible: a student who hits the rare M002-carry-forward race (saveState rejection on the rung-3 commit) gets told 'review and edit further' when their code is actually correct. Low frequency in practice (EACCES on .sui-deepbook-course is rare on a dev box) but the agent narration is the wrong contract on the page.
recommendation: Change the bullet to 'If the auto-verify passed (autoVerifyResult.pass: true), announce success. If autoVerifyResult.advanced is also true, the cursor advanced; if pass=true but advanced=false, the verification passed but state failed to persist — surface autoVerifyResult.output and the structured error to the student and ask them to resolve the persistence issue.' Treat pass and advanced as orthogonal in the prose, mirroring how the contract treats them.
evidence:
- If the auto-verify passed (`autoVerifyResult.advanced: true`), announce success and proceed.
  - If the auto-verify failed (`autoVerifyResult.advanced: false`), encourage the student to review and edit further, then call `verifySpot` again.

title: spot.rungs path resolution has dual-mode ambiguity (manifest path vs canonical fallback)
description: requestHint reads the rung markdown via two competing schemes: (a) `spot.rungs?.hint_md` (relative manifest path), (b) fallback to `paths/<slug>/rungs/<spot.id>/<file>` (canonical). The schema in mcp/server/src/schemas/phases.ts declares `rungs?: SpotRungs` as optional and validates the three fields are strings when present. The fixture phases.json populates both — `rungs.hint_md = 'rungs/p1-spot-1/hint.md'` is byte-identical to what the canonical fallback would produce. So the fallback branch is always dead in production; requestHint is paying for two code paths to express one decision. Worse, if a future curriculum author omits the rungs field in phases.json (which the schema permits), requestHint silently falls back to the canonical path — but the contract was specific about the rungs declaration being required for routing. Dead path or undocumented fallback; pick one.
impact: Cognitive load on the next maintainer (which path is canonical?), and a silent contract gap: if rungs is omitted from phases.json the rung-content-missing error surface depends on whether the fallback path happens to also exist. Tests don't cover the fallback at all — T-031 renames auto.md (the canonical path), so it exercises the fallback only when spot.rungs?.auto_write_md happens to point at the same file.
recommendation: Either (a) make spot.rungs required in the schema (validatePhases enforces it, the fallback is removed, requestHint reads only spot.rungs.hint_md/reference_md/auto_write_md), or (b) drop the spot.rungs field entirely (always derive paths from spot.id). The current 'optional with fallback' state is the worst of both. If keeping the fallback for back-compat, add a code comment explicitly framing it as such and a test that proves the fallback fires when rungs is omitted.

title: rung-3 casts spot to SpotData without stub-spot defensive check that nextSpot has
description: nextSpot.ts:91-98 explicitly defends against stub spots by checking `target_file === undefined || target_range === undefined || prompt === undefined || verification === undefined` and returning a structured error before consuming the spot. requestHint's rung-3 path lacks this defense: line 240 casts `spot as SpotData` and runAutoWrite immediately uses `spot.target_file!` (non-null assertion at ladder.ts:133). If the manifest defines a stub spot (legitimate per the schema, where target_file/target_range can be absent for navigation-only spots), rung-3 invocation against that spot produces a TypeError 'Cannot read properties of undefined' at the path.join line — not a structured error. Tests pass because the only fixture spot (p1-spot-1) has the full shape.
impact: First curriculum that ships a stub spot crashes the rung-3 call with a TypeError that propagates to the MCP boundary as a 'tool error'. Future-proofing gap. Also: the cast `spot as SpotData` is semantically a lie — SpotStub | SpotData is a real union in the schema, and the cast tells TS to ignore the discrimination requirement.
recommendation: Mirror nextSpot.ts's stub-spot guard at requestHint.ts after the getCurrentSpot call: if rung is 3 and spot.target_file/target_range/verification are undefined, return `{ ok: false, error: { kind: 'no-active-spot' or a new 'stub-spot-cannot-auto-write', message: '...' } }`. For rungs 1 and 2 which only need the rung markdown, no defense is required.
evidence: autoWriteResult = await runAutoWrite(projectRoot, spot as SpotData, payload);

title: harness verifyStub closure variable type widens silently after withVerifyStub call
description: verifyStub is declared `let verifyStub: { pass: boolean; output?: string } | null = null;`, then both verifySpot and requestHint intercepts copy it to a const local (`const stub = verifyStub;`) inside their `if (toolName === 'verifySpot' && verifyStub !== null)` blocks. The two intercept blocks are byte-identical aside from the tool name and the inner runRequestHint vs runVerifySpot call — same closure variable, same narrowing pattern, same VerifySpawnFn shim, same MCP-envelope wrapping. The second intercept (requestHint) was clearly copy-pasted from the first (verifySpot). Both also share the bug where if the test sets `withVerifyStub({ pass: false })` and only the rung-3 dispatch fires, the callTool intercept rewrites the verify-bound stub for both tools, so the test cannot independently stub verifySpot's first call vs rung-3's nested verifySpot call (both see pass:false). The harness logic technically composes, but it doesn't compose cleanly — it duplicates.
impact: 30 lines of pure duplication in a file that is the test infrastructure for the entire engine surface. Every future tool that wants verify-stub interception will paste a third copy. The shared-stub semantics is also under-documented: tests that need 'verifySpot fails, but rung-3's nested verifySpot passes' to exercise the M002 race can't be expressed with the current harness API.
recommendation: Extract `interceptVerifyStub(toolName, args, runner)` returning the MCP envelope, parameterized by the tool's own runner function. Both intercept blocks become 4-line calls. Optionally, replace the single `verifyStub` field with a per-tool map (`verifyStubs: Map<'verifySpot' | 'requestHint', StubResult>`) so independent stubbing is possible — that would unblock the M002 race test and tighten the rung-3 'auto-verify failed but second verifySpot passes' E-004 scenario.
